_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Allen County, Kentucky. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses for the upper Barren River valley county — formed 1815 from Barren and Warren counties, with Scottsville as county seat. Allen County experienced a courthouse fire in 1902 that destroyed many records, making it one of the more difficult Pennyroyal counties for early genealogical research.

previous_courthouses.location: Scottsville — original frame courthouse on the public square
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by a brick structure. Records transferred.

previous_courthouses.location: Scottsville — brick courthouse on the public square
previous_courthouses.fate: Destroyed by fire in 1902. Significant record losses. The fire was one of the most devastating courthouse fires in the Pennyroyal.

previous_courthouses.location: Scottsville — rebuilt courthouse on the same site
previous_courthouses.fate: Post-fire reconstruction. Records from this era are well-preserved. Replaced by the current 1957 building.

record_losses.cause: Courthouse fire — accidental fire destroyed the courthouse and many records
records_survived: some deed books (volumes that had been removed or were stored in fireproof safes)
record_losses.description: The 1902 Allen County courthouse fire was a catastrophic loss for genealogical research. The fire destroyed the courthouse building and much of its contents, including most of the earliest deed books, will books, and court order books dating back to the county's 1815 formation. Some records survived — volumes that had been temporarily removed from the building, records stored in a fireproof safe, and some later-era records. The surviving record set is fragmentary for the pre-1870 period. Researchers working with Allen County families before the Civil War must rely heavily on substitute sources.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The 1902 fire means that {ancestor_name}'s earliest Allen County records may not exist. If the family was in Allen County before the Civil War, the trail through the courthouse is broken. Virginia land grants, federal census records, and parent county records (Barren and Warren) become essential substitutes. Post-1870 records survived in better condition.
record_losses.source: Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: Kentucky County Records. Frankfort, 1937-1942.

deeds.completeness: Poor to fair for early period — many early deed books were destroyed in the 1902 fire. Post-1870 deeds are more complete. Some early volumes survived in fragmentary condition.
deeds.gaps: Significant gaps in the 1815-1870 period. Researchers must cross-reference with Virginia land grants, parent county deeds, and adjacent county records.
deeds.repository: Allen County Clerk, Scottsville; KDLA microfilm (for surviving records)
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm (limited); Ancestry.com Kentucky land records (partial)

wills_estates.completeness: Poor for early period — most early will books were destroyed in 1902. Post-1870 records are more complete.
wills_estates.gaps: Severe gaps in the 1815-1870 period. Estate settlements from this era are largely lost.

marriages.completeness: Fair — some early marriage bonds survived the fire; the series is more complete after 1870
marriages.gaps: Gaps in the earliest decades. Some bonds survived because they had been transcribed or published.

court_minutes.completeness: Poor for early period — most court order books pre-1860 were destroyed in 1902
court_minutes.gaps: Severe gaps before 1860. Post-Civil War records are more complete.

vital_records.completeness: Kentucky mandated registration in 1852, but compliance in rural Allen County was very poor until approximately 1911. The pre-1911 registers are fragmentary.
vital_records.repository: Kentucky Office of Vital Statistics, Frankfort; KDLA for pre-1911 registers

substitute_sources.source_type: Barren and Warren County parent records (pre-1815)
substitute_sources.description: Allen County was formed from Barren and Warren counties in 1815. All records before that date are in the parent counties. Both Barren and Warren counties have relatively well-preserved early records.
substitute_sources.repository: Barren County Clerk, Glasgow; Warren County Clerk, Bowling Green; KDLA microfilm
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for any family in the Scottsville area before 1815. Warren County records (intact from 1797) and Barren County records (mostly intact despite the 1864 fire) are the primary substitute sources.
substitute_sources.source: Jillson, Willard Rouse. The Kentucky Land Grants. Louisville: Standard Printing Company, 1925.

substitute_sources.description: Kentucky land grants issued under Virginia authority before 1792. The Kentucky Land Office holds the original grant records.
substitute_sources.repository: Kentucky Secretary of State, Land Office Division; Library of Virginia
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical when Allen County deed books are missing. The original Virginia grant records document the first land claims in the area and often record the grantee's Virginia county of origin.
substitute_sources.source: Jillson, Willard Rouse. Old Kentucky Entries and Deeds. Louisville: Standard Printing Company, 1926.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1820-1940. Agricultural and slave schedules (1850-1880). Allen County is enumerated from the 1820 census.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critically important because the 1902 fire makes census records the primary surviving documentation for many Allen County families before 1870. The 1850 and 1860 censuses, with their agricultural and slave schedules, may be the richest surviving source for antebellum Allen County.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Allen County, KY.

substitute_sources.description: Baptist and Methodist congregations served Allen County from its earliest years. Church minute books may survive where courthouse records do not.
substitute_sources.repository: Southern Baptist Theological Seminary Archives, Louisville; individual congregations; Kentucky Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Particularly valuable given the 1902 courthouse fire. Baptist minute books record births, marriages, deaths, disciplinary actions, and dismissal letters that may be the only surviving documentation for some families.
substitute_sources.source: Spencer, J. H. A History of Kentucky Baptists from 1769 to 1885. Cincinnati: J. R. Baumes, 1885.

substitute_sources.description: Allen County borders Macon County, Tennessee. Families moved back and forth across the state line.
substitute_sources.repository: Macon County Clerk, Lafayette, TN; Tennessee State Library and Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Cross-referencing with Macon County records can locate families that moved across the state line. Before Macon County's 1842 formation, the Tennessee records are in Smith and Sumner counties.
substitute_sources.source: Tennessee State Library and Archives. Guide to County Records on Microfilm.

narrative_hooks: The Allen County courthouse burned in 1902 and took most of the early records with it — if {ancestor_name} was in Allen County before the Civil War, the deed that proved it may no longer exist, and the census is likely the only surviving document with {pronoun_possessive} name.

When the Allen County courthouse burned, the Barren and Warren county records became the lifeline — {ancestor_name}'s family was likely in one of those parent counties before Allen was carved out in 1815, and those earlier records survived.

Allen County sits on the Tennessee border, and {ancestor_name} may have crossed the state line as casually as crossing a creek — Macon County, Tennessee, records may hold the other half of the family's story.

The 1850 census for Allen County may be the most important surviving document for antebellum families — it names every person in the household, and with the courthouse records gone, it is often the oldest document with {ancestor_name}'s name on it.
source_references: Jillson, Willard Rouse. The Kentucky Land Grants. Louisville: Standard Printing Company, 1925.
Jillson, Willard Rouse. Old Kentucky Entries and Deeds. Louisville: Standard Printing Company, 1926.
Spencer, J. H. A History of Kentucky Baptists from 1769 to 1885. Cincinnati: J. R. Baumes, 1885.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: Kentucky County Records. Frankfort, 1937-1942.
Kentucky Department for Libraries and Archives. Guide to Kentucky County Records on Microfilm. Frankfort, various editions.
